Row1.Rationale: Cetuximab blocks ligand binding and dimerization of EGFR, suppressing autophosphorylation and downstream MAPK and PI3K–AKT–mTOR cascades that drive proliferation, survival, angiogenesis, and invasion in EGFR‑dependent NSCLC; tumors in which these pathways are durably downregulated are most likely to benefit.

Row2.Rationale: EGFR activates MAPK signaling to promote proliferation and survival in NSCLC.[2,8,18] Activating KRAS or BRAF mutations, or bypass activation via AXL, MET or other RTKs, maintain ERK signaling despite EGFR inhibition and are established mediators of primary or acquired resistance to EGFR‑directed agents, including cetuximab.

Row3.Rationale: EGFR and multiple RTKs converge on PI3K–AKT–mTOR, which upregulates anti‑apoptotic proteins and supports growth.[2,8,12,18,20] PIK3CA mutations, PTEN loss, or activation of MET, HER2/HER3, or AXL sustain AKT signaling when EGFR is blocked, uncoupling survival from EGFR and diminishing cetuximab efficacy.

Row4.Rationale: EGFR engages JAK–STAT signaling, and constitutive STAT3 activity (e.g., EGFR–STAT3–Bcl‑xL axis) promotes survival and drug resistance in NSCLC.[8,12,16,18] Bypass activation of JAK–STAT through inflammatory cytokines or alternative RTKs can maintain prosurvival transcription under EGFR inhibition, limiting cetuximab benefit.

Row5.Rationale: Cetuximab blocks EGFR ligand binding and dimerization with HER2/HER3, attenuating potent heterodimer signaling through MAPK and PI3K–AKT.[1,2,8,14,17–19,21] Upregulation or amplification of HER2/HER3, or increased EGFR‑family ligands (e.g., TGF‑α, amphiregulin, HB‑EGF), restores ErbB heterodimer activity and drives resistance in EGFR‑dependent lung tumors.

Row6.Rationale: MET amplification or HGF overexpression activates MET, which sustains ERBB3, PI3K–AKT and MAPK signaling even when EGFR is blocked.[3,5,15,17,20] MET‑driven bypass is a major resistance mechanism to EGFR TKIs and has been observed in cetuximab‑resistant models, supporting combined EGFR/MET targeting in biomarker‑defined NSCLC subsets.

Row7.Rationale: In NSCLC models, acquired cetuximab resistance is associated with strong AXL overexpression/activation and tight AXL–EGFR association; AXL maintains EGFR phosphorylation and MAPK signaling via a positive feedback loop involving c‑Jun, and its inhibition reduces proliferation and downstream signaling, partially restoring EGFR dependence.[4], [7]

Row8.Pathway ID/Name: Nuclear EGFR & Src family kinase (SFK)–EGFR Y845 axis
Row8.Rationale: Cetuximab can induce EGFR nuclear translocation, particularly when combined with radiation; SFK‑mediated phosphorylation at EGFR Y845 promotes nuclear trafficking.[6] Nuclear EGFR acts as a transcriptional regulator (e.g., cyclin D1, COX‑2) and kinase supporting DNA repair and cell‑cycle progression, thereby attenuating the antiproliferative and radiosensitizing effects of cetuximab.

Row9.Rationale: EGFR and other RTKs can activate NF‑κB, which induces anti‑apoptotic genes and cytokines that promote survival, EMT, and immune evasion.[3,8,11,13,15,19] Chemotherapy‑induced HB‑EGF and NF‑κB activation increase EGFR ligand production, fostering resistance to EGFR‑targeted agents; persistent NF‑κB signaling under cetuximab maintains prosurvival programs.

Row10.Rationale: Genomic profiling of EGFR‑driven tumors (largely NSCLC) shows frequent Wnt/β‑catenin pathway alterations, often more common than PI3K–AKT–mTOR lesions.[13] Wnt/β‑catenin activation is linked to EGFR‑mutant NSCLC, gefitinib resistance, EMT and cancer stem‑like phenotypes; Wnt inhibition can re‑sensitize cells to EGFR inhibitors, implying that persistent Wnt signaling can blunt cetuximab benefit.

Row11.Rationale: Epithelial–mesenchymal transition is associated with loss of epithelial markers, increased invasiveness, and broad drug resistance; EMT‑like states are recognized mechanisms of acquired resistance to EGFR TKIs in NSCLC and are expected to reduce dependence on EGFR signaling and promote escape from cetuximab‑mediated EGFR blockade.

Row12.Rationale: EGFR‑inhibition–induced radiosensitization depends on intact p53‑mediated G1 arrest and apoptosis; cetuximab‑resistant cells show reduced p53 function with concomitant ERK1/2 upregulation, indicating that defective p53 signaling and impaired checkpoint control diminish apoptotic responses to EGFR blockade in NSCLC.
